exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.title: A: Use the word given in capitals to form a word
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals at the end of each line to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.passage: Daniel Marsh, the (1) ...... in a fraud case that has drawn considerable media coverage, was yesterday found guilty of art theft on a large scale. The police (2) ...... spanned eighteen months and involved officers from three different cities. During the hearing, over sixty hours of (3) ...... were presented to the court. Marsh's (4) ...... had argued that their client was overseas during the period in question, but they could not provide the necessary (5) ...... . Officers described Marsh as a highly experienced (6) ...... who had operated across Europe and was believed to be responsible for a series of (7) ...... from private galleries over several years. This is not Marsh's first (8) ...... . A decade earlier, he was found guilty of (9) ...... and received a two-year suspended sentence. The judge is due to announce the length of Marsh's (10) ...... at the next hearing.

items.sentence: The councillors decided to bring **G** in a law restricting the use of loud music in residential areas.
items.answer: G – in a law restricting the use of loud music in residential areas.
acceptedAnswers: G – in a law restricting the use of loud music in residential areas.

items.sentence: Detectives are urging anyone who saw the incident to come **D** forward with any information they feel could help the investigation.
items.answer: D – forward with any information they feel could help the investigation.
acceptedAnswers: D – forward with any information they feel could help the investigation.

items.sentence: The security guard was convinced the alarm had gone **A** off accidentally because of a fault in the wiring.
items.answer: A – off accidentally because of a fault in the wiring.
acceptedAnswers: A – off accidentally because of a fault in the wiring.
items.explanation: Alarm phrasal verb → had gone **off** accidentally.

items.sentence: The officer began to take **H** down everything the witness described, noting every small detail.
items.answer: H – down everything the witness described, noting every small detail.
acceptedAnswers: H – down everything the witness described, noting every small detail.
items.explanation: Record in writing → take **down** everything the witness described.

items.sentence: A number of elderly residents have been taken **C** in by this scheme and have handed over significant amounts of money.
items.answer: C – in by this scheme and have handed over significant amounts of money.
acceptedAnswers: C – in by this scheme and have handed over significant amounts of money.
items.explanation: Deceived by → taken **in** by this scheme.

items.sentence: Sanders was convicted of holding **E** up a security van and was given a seven-year prison sentence.
items.answer: E – up a security van and was given a seven-year prison sentence.
acceptedAnswers: E – up a security van and was given a seven-year prison sentence.
items.explanation: Robbery phrasal verb → holding **up** a security van.

items.sentence: Far too many people get **B** away with minor offences because the courts are already overloaded.
items.answer: B – away with minor offences because the courts are already overloaded.
acceptedAnswers: B – away with minor offences because the courts are already overloaded.
items.explanation: Escape punishment → get **away** with minor offences.

items.sentence: The magistrate decided to let Kevin **F** off with a warning, but made it clear there would be no second chances.
items.answer: F – off with a warning, but made it clear there would be no second chances.
acceptedAnswers: F – off with a warning, but made it clear there would be no second chances.
items.explanation: Lenient punishment → let Kevin **off** with a warning.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Detectives said they had not seen a crime as bad as this in over ten years. **the**
Detectives described it ...... over ten years.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative comparison, so the correct answer is "as the worst crime in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "as the worst crime in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Some people don't respect the law. **have**
Some people ...... the law.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no respect, so the correct answer is "have no respect for".

wrongOptions.no respect for: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have no respect for", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
In certain nations, governments have laws so strict that people have very little freedom. **such**
In certain nations, governments ...... people have very little freedom.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is such…that structure, so the correct answer is "have such strict laws that".

wrongOptions.such strict laws that: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have such strict laws that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
The teenager was too young to be sent to an adult prison. **old**
The teenager ...... to be sent to an adult prison.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not old enough, so the correct answer is "was not old enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was not old enough", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The bystander thought I was the thief, but quickly realised her mistake. **for**
The bystander ...... but quickly realised her mistake.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is mistook someone for, so the correct answer is "took me for the thief".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "took me for the thief", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
Nobody in the country knows the law as well as Mr Parkhurst. **than**
Mr Parkhurst knows the law ...... in the country.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is superlative, so the correct answer is "better than anyone else".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "better than anyone else", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
There have been so many robberies lately that residents have stopped going out after dark. **such**
There have been ...... robberies lately that residents have stopped going out after dark.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is quantity + that, so the correct answer is "such a lot of".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "such a lot of", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Even though we weren't old enough, we managed to talk our way past the bouncer. **too**
Even though we ...... we managed to talk our way past the bouncer.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is too + adjective, so the correct answer is "were too young".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "were too young", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The judge told the jury to consider the defendant's personal history. **account**
The judge told the jury to ...... the defendant's personal history.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is fixed phrase, so the correct answer is "take into account".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "take into account", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.
